Handbook of Practical Program Evaluation Chapter Map

Chapter-level reading map for Handbook of Practical Program Evaluation.

Source

This map organises the generated chapter notes for Handbook of Practical Program Evaluation (Newcomer, Hatry, and Wholey 2015).

Reading Route

  • Plan useful evaluations: Chapters 1-12 cover stakeholder engagement, logic models, exploratory evaluation, monitoring, causal designs, case studies, multisite work, community change, and cultural responsiveness.
  • Collect practical data: Chapters 13-21 cover records, surveys, role playing, observation, fieldwork, Internet tools, interviews, focus groups, and stories.
  • Analyze and synthesize: Chapters 22-25 cover qualitative analysis, statistics, economic analysis, and evidence synthesis.
  • Support use: Chapters 26-31 cover pitfalls, recommendations, writing, contracting, government use, quality, ethics, and trends.

Chapters

Chapter Source Pages Main Design Question Link
Chapter 1: Planning and Designing Useful Evaluations 49-78 Useful evaluation design starts by matching information needs, context, rigor, and use before methods are chosen. chapter-01-planning-and-designing-useful-evaluations.qmd
Chapter 2: Analyzing and Engaging Stakeholders 79-103 Stakeholder analysis is a design task that clarifies intended users, influence, interests, and feasible engagement. chapter-02-analyzing-and-engaging-stakeholders.qmd
Chapter 3: Using Logic Models 104-129 Logic models organize program components, assumptions, outcomes, and contextual factors so evaluation questions can be focused. chapter-03-using-logic-models.qmd
Chapter 4: Exploratory Evaluation 130-149 Exploratory evaluation improves feasibility and usefulness when program goals, boundaries, data, or uses are unclear. chapter-04-exploratory-evaluation.qmd
Chapter 5: Performance Measurement 150-177 Performance measurement systems monitor outcomes and operations so managers can improve programs without waiting for episodic studies. chapter-05-performance-measurement.qmd
Chapter 6: Comparison Group Designs 178-213 Comparison group designs estimate program impact when randomized assignment is not feasible but credible counterfactual evidence is still needed. chapter-06-comparison-group-designs.qmd
Chapter 7: Randomized Controlled Trials 214-237 Randomized controlled trials strengthen causal claims when assignment, implementation, ethics, and operations can support the design. chapter-07-randomized-controlled-trials.qmd
Chapter 8: Case Studies 238-267 Case studies answer descriptive and explanatory questions by examining programs in context. chapter-08-case-studies.qmd
Chapter 9: Recruitment and Retention of Study Participants 238-267 Recruitment and retention are design-critical because missing or unrepresentative participants can undermine evidence quality. chapter-09-recruitment-and-retention-of-study-participants.qmd
Chapter 10: Designing, Managing, and Analyzing Multisite Evaluations 268-302 Multisite evaluation requires coordinated design, site-level management, and cross-site analysis. chapter-10-designing-managing-and-analyzing-multisite-evaluations.qmd
Chapter 11: Evaluating Community Change Programs 303-330 Community change evaluation must handle complex interventions, shifting context, multiple actors, and long time horizons. chapter-11-evaluating-community-change-programs.qmd
Chapter 12: Culturally Responsive Evaluation 331-360 Culturally responsive evaluation embeds cultural context, equity, and stakeholder voice in evaluation design and use. chapter-12-culturally-responsive-evaluation.qmd
Chapter 13: Using Agency Records 369-388 Agency and administrative records are common evaluation data sources, but access, definitions, missingness, and quality must be assessed. chapter-13-using-agency-records.qmd
Chapter 14: Using Surveys 389-424 Surveys provide structured evidence from clients, households, agencies, or other populations when questions, samples, and response processes are well designed. chapter-14-using-surveys.qmd
Chapter 15: Role Playing 425-453 Role playing uses trained testers to observe transactions and service quality that may otherwise be hidden. chapter-15-role-playing.qmd
Chapter 16: Using Ratings by Trained Observers 454-486 Trained observer ratings turn direct observation into systematic evidence through explicit scales and procedures. chapter-16-using-ratings-by-trained-observers.qmd
Chapter 17: Collecting Data in the Field 487-515 Field data collection turns evaluation questions into systematic on-site evidence about implementation, quality, context, and outcomes. chapter-17-collecting-data-in-the-field.qmd
Chapter 18: Using the Internet 516-538 Internet-based tools support literature review, online data collection, project communication, and evidence dissemination. chapter-18-using-the-internet.qmd
Chapter 19: Semi-Structured Interviewing 539-546 Semi-structured interviews use guides and probes to collect comparable but open-ended evidence. chapter-19-semi-structured-interviewing.qmd
Chapter 20: Focus Group Interviewing 547-576 Focus groups collect interactive qualitative evidence that can support design, interpretation, and understanding of stakeholder experience. chapter-20-focus-group-interviewing.qmd
Chapter 21: Using Stories in Evaluation 577-598 Stories can make evaluation findings vivid and memorable when collected and presented systematically. chapter-21-using-stories-in-evaluation.qmd
Chapter 22: Qualitative Data Analysis 605-638 Qualitative analysis turns field notes, documents, observations, and interviews into credible patterns, explanations, and findings. chapter-22-qualitative-data-analysis.qmd
Chapter 23: Using Statistics in Evaluation 639-684 Statistical analysis helps evaluators draw clearer conclusions when sampling, measurement, assumptions, and reporting are handled carefully. chapter-23-using-statistics-in-evaluation.qmd
Chapter 24: Cost-Effectiveness and Cost-Benefit Analysis 685-716 Economic analysis compares program costs with effects or monetized benefits so decision makers can judge value. chapter-24-cost-effectiveness-and-cost-benefit-analysis.qmd
Chapter 25: Meta-Analyses, Systematic Reviews, and Evaluation Syntheses 717-740 Evidence synthesis clarifies where evaluation evidence is strong, absent, ambiguous, or not comparable. chapter-25-meta-analyses-systematic-reviews-and-evaluation-syntheses.qmd
Chapter 26: Pitfalls in Evaluations 745-766 Evaluation pitfalls are predictable design, data, analysis, and use problems that can be anticipated and reduced. chapter-26-pitfalls-in-evaluations.qmd
Chapter 27: Providing Recommendations, Suggestions, and Options for Improvement 767-780 Recommendations are most useful when they flow from findings, address causes or effects, and give decision makers feasible options. chapter-27-providing-recommendations-suggestions-and-options-for-improvement.qmd
Chapter 28: Writing for Impact 781-805 Evaluation writing should convey a clear message, credible evidence, useful methods detail, and formats suited to intended audiences. chapter-28-writing-for-impact.qmd
Chapter 29: Evaluation Contracting 807-830 Evaluation contracting translates information needs into feasible plans, RFPs, contractor selection, monitoring, and usable products. chapter-29-evaluation-contracting.qmd
Chapter 30: Use of Evaluation in Government 831-857 Government evaluation use depends on politics, bureaucracy, evidence agendas, and strategies for overcoming institutional barriers. chapter-30-use-of-evaluation-in-government.qmd
Chapter 31: Evaluation Challenges, Issues, and Trends 858-870 The concluding chapter identifies cross-cutting challenges in evaluation quality, evaluator capacity, standards, ethics, and evidence use. chapter-31-evaluation-challenges-issues-and-trends.qmd

Legacy Extracts

  • Evaluation Planning and Design
  • Planning and Designing Useful Evaluations
  • Performance Measurement and Program Evaluation

References

Newcomer, Kathryn E., Harry P. Hatry, and Joseph S. Wholey. 2015. Handbook of Practical Program Evaluation. Jossey-Bass.
